key_insight: GrapNet treats the neural network itself as an editable graph program, so relations, freezing, routing, and backend changes can be handled structurally instead of by ad-hoc parameter surgery.
engineering_relevance: This is useful for engineering systems that need modular, auditable, and reconfigurable models, especially when combining learned components with fixed feature extractors or changing network topology over time.
method: Child-owned graph nodes store next-layer references plus aligned trainable allocation vectors.
Structural rules and execution policies are separated from node internals, enabling growth, freezing, relation deletion, grouping, and attention-based routing.
The same graph can be lowered into dense snapshots once topology stabilizes and can interface with dense layers, CNNs, ResNets, attention blocks, and transformers.
evidence_signal: In matched ten-seed experiments, GrapNet+ER outperformed a larger dense MLP+ER on Split Fashion-MNIST by 12.08 points (63.16% vs 51.08%, p=1.3e-5) and improved a Split CIFAR-10 online head by 3.81 points over MLP-256 (p=0.0026).
watch_item: Because the substrate changes topology during training, careful implementation is needed to preserve optimizer state, execution correctness, and fair comparisons across different structural views.
paper_title: GrapNet: A Programmable Dynamic-Architecture Neural Graph Substrate
